Some people are calling for Arman Tsarukyan to be punished after the incident at Real American Freestyle — but Chael Sonnen says that argument completely misses the point. In combat sports, emotions run high and things can get chaotic. While Chael agrees that rules matter and unsportsmanlike behavior shouldn’t be ignored, he questions why so many fans are demanding punishment for another grown adult. Chael explains that Arman Tsarukyan has already faced consequences throughout his recent career. After withdrawing from a fight due to injury, Tsarukyan stepped up as a backup fighter and went through an entire training camp without competing. He also dealt with fallout from the Dan Hooker fight, where an accidental head clash broke Hooker’s nose and affected Tsarukyan’s title shot opportunity. The bigger point Chael makes is about how punishment works in combat sports. If a promotion releases a fighter or pushes them away, they’re not really punishing them — they’re often just sending them to a different promotion where they can continue competing. Chael also revisits stories involving Dana White, Conor McGregor, Jon Jones, and Anderson Silva, explaining why promotions rarely handle controversy the way fans expect. articles.title: Can Tom Aspinall Stop the UFC From Stripping Him?
articles.description: Tom Aspinall may be about to lose his UFC heavyweight title — and the clock could already be ticking. Chael Sonnen breaks down the growing speculation that the UFC could strip Tom Aspinall of the interim heavyweight championship if he doesn’t defend it soon. In MMA, the rule is simple: if a champion isn’t defending the belt, the promotion can move on. But what happens if the champion suddenly says he’s ready? Aspinall recently posted that he’s “back to business,” which raises an important question: if he’s healthy and ready to fight, can the UFC still strip him? Chael explains the potential chess match between fighter and promotion. If Aspinall publicly signals he’s ready to compete, the UFC would likely need to offer him a fight before moving forward with another title bout. Chael also shares a bold prediction — Alex Pereira moving up to heavyweight to fight for the belt, possibly against Ciryl Gane, potentially at a massive event. But if Aspinall truly is ready, that entire plan could change overnight. The real question becomes: did Aspinall make a strategic move… or just send a message that means nothing? Because if he’s truly ready, the UFC may have to give him the fight instead of taking the belt. Charles Oliveira vs Max Holloway is one of the most fascinating matchups in MMA right now — and Chael Sonnen has an official prediction. The two fighters met once before earlier in their careers, but the circumstances were completely different. It was fought at a different weight class and over three rounds, which Chael argues dramatically changes the dynamics compared to a five-round championship fight. Max Holloway is known for being one of the toughest fighters in the sport, especially in rounds four and five, where many opponents say the pace becomes unbearable. Because of that, many fans believe Holloway should be the favorite in a longer fight. But Chael believes people may be overlooking one critical factor: Charles Oliveira’s wrestling. Oliveira has repeatedly shown that when he needs a takedown, he can find one — something he demonstrated against fighters like Tony Ferguson and Michael Chandler. If Oliveira can secure just a few key takedowns across five rounds, he may be able to steal rounds and control the fight. Chael also weighs in on the BMF championship stakes and explains why the winner should defend that belt instead of immediately chasing another title fight. So who wins the rematch? articles.title: Eddie Hearn's Making a Big Mistake...
articles.description: Chael Sonnen breaks down the debate between two of combat sports’ biggest promoters: Eddie Hearn and Dana White. After Eddie Hearn suggested that he cares more about his fighters because he shows emotion after victories, Chael explains why that comparison misses a crucial difference between boxing and MMA. In boxing, promoters represent individual fighters who compete against fighters promoted by other companies. In the UFC model, the organization promotes the entire roster, meaning Dana White’s fighters are often competing against other fighters under the same banner. Chael uses his background in wrestling to explain why that changes everything when it comes to showing emotion, picking sides, and promoting fights. The discussion also touches on recent tensions between Hearn and the UFC, including the signing of Conor Benn and what it says about the competitive landscape between boxing promotions and MMA organizations. Chael argues that promoters are ultimately competing just like fighters, and sometimes showing too much emotion publicly can weaken your position in that battle. In this breakdown, Chael explains why the promotional models are fundamentally different and why Dana White’s approach may ultimately be the more effective strategy in the fight business. #UFC #DanaWhite #EddieHearn #ChaelSonnen #boxingBad Guy Inc merch now available at https://BadGuyInc.com Click here to subscribe: https://bit.ly/3FrHIPK Follow me on IG: https://bit.ly/3HY7BIE Follow me on Twitter: https://bit.ly/3tvY5J7 Follow me on Threads https://www.threads.com/@sonnench

articles.title: Chael: They’re Stripping Tom Aspinall This Week
articles.description: Chael Sonnen believes a major shakeup is coming to the UFC heavyweight division—and it could happen very soon. According to Chael, the UFC may strip Tom Aspinall of the interim heavyweight championship as the promotion prepares to announce fights tied to the rumored White House card. Chael explains that historically when a sitting champion moves divisions, they immediately fight for another title rather than taking a lesser opportunity. That history could mean Alex Pereira moves directly into a heavyweight title fight, potentially against Ciryl Gane, while Aspinall is temporarily removed from the championship picture. Chael argues that while stripping a champion might sound harsh, it could actually benefit Aspinall long-term by setting him up for a bigger return. If Aspinall comes back to reclaim the belt later, he would have the opportunity to become a two-time UFC heavyweight champion, a distinction that carries major legacy value. Chael breaks down why the UFC might make this decision, how the heavyweight title situation has developed around Jon Jones, and why the promotion may be setting up a new championship fight in the near future. articles.title: Come Up With a Better Lie, Giorgio
articles.description: Chael Sonnen reacts to a strange story involving Giorgio and Arman Tsarukyan that quickly unraveled. A grappling match between the two was reportedly scheduled on extremely short notice, only to be canceled almost immediately after it was announced. The explanation? Giorgio claimed he needed to speak with Dana White about possibly fighting Arman in the UFC instead. Chael isn’t buying it. In this breakdown, Chael explains why the timeline doesn’t make sense and why the explanation creates more questions than answers. According to Chael, Giorgio’s team was still trying to get Dana White’s contact information after the interview was already published, making the story difficult to believe. Chael also points out the bigger issue in combat sports: credibility. When fighters make announcements or callouts that never materialize, it hurts their word with both fans and promoters. Chael argues that if Giorgio wanted to keep the attention and momentum going, there was a smarter way to frame the situation. Instead of canceling a grappling match and claiming UFC negotiations, the conflict could have stayed within the Real American Freestyle wrestling storyline where it originally began. Chael breaks down why the situation is unusual, why Arman Tsarukyan realistically wouldn’t be fighting outside the UFC right now, and why messaging matters just as much as performance in today’s fight business. #UFC #ChaelSonnen #ArmanTsarukyan #MMA 00:00 The Giorgio story begins 00:30 Grappling match scheduled… then canceled 01:00 The Dana White claim 01:45 Why the timeline doesn’t add up 02:15 Why Arman wouldn’t take that fight 03:00 The credibility problem 03:30 Chael’s advice: tell a better story Bad Guy Inc merch now available at https://BadGuyInc.com Click here to subscribe: https://bit.ly/3FrHIPK Follow me on IG: https://bit.ly/3HY7BIE Follow me on Twitter: https://bit.ly/3tvY5J7 Follow me on Threads https://www.threads.com/@sonnench

The billion-dollar headlines, the half-a-billion-dollar Mayweather vs. McGregor numbers, the outrageous pay-per-view claims — none of them have ever been confirmed by publicly traded distributors. No executive from Showtime, DirecTV, Time Warner, or Cox has ever verified those figures. In boxing, the biggest pay-per-view is often just the biggest press release. Floyd’s now claiming financial disputes with Showtime while headlines swirl about money owed to Logan Paul — and that contradiction matters. In boxing, the promoter and the fighter roles blur, and financial chaos is common. Add in multiple exhibition bookings — Manny Pacquiao, Mike Tyson, and more — and it starts looking less like “legacy fights” and more like necessity. This isn’t hate. This is economics. If Floyd truly had generational wealth sitting untouched, he wouldn’t be juggling exhibition dates across continents. The bigger question isn’t whether Floyd is broke — it’s why we ever believed the numbers in the first place. #FloydMayweather #Boxing #UFC #LoganPaul #MannyPacquiao #MikeTyson 0:00 Is Floyd Broke? 0:19 Why Do You Go to Work? 1:06 Boxing Pay-Per-View Myth 2:04 Who Wrote The Check? 2:41 The Logan Paul Issue 3:47 Boxing Economics Explained 4:45 Three Fights Scheduled 5:32 Pacquiao Complication 6:15 The Tyson Problem 7:06 Standard of the Industry 7:24 The Real Story Bad Guy Inc merch now available at https://BadGuyInc.com Click here to subscribe: https://bit.ly/3FrHIPK Follow me on IG: https://bit.ly/3HY7BIE Follow me on Twitter: https://bit.ly/3tvY5J7 Follow me on Threads https://www.threads.com/@sonnench
